what_it_does: Documents the current Tailscale tailnet and serve/funnel configuration on a WSL2 workstation, then prescribes a concrete, non-destructive way to expose OpenWebUI to an iPhone over auto-TLS HTTPS using a new tailscale serve port.
observations: Tailscale daemon runs natively inside WSL2, so serve targets must be reachable at 127.0.0.1 inside the same WSL instance.
OpenWebUI is not currently running; ollama is running on 127.0.0.1:11434 inside WSL.
A stale half-open listener occupies *:8080 and refuses connections, with no visible owning PID.
Recommended command is `tailscale serve --bg --https=8444 localhost:8081` after running OpenWebUI on 127.0.0.1:8081.
The file classifies evidence as Verified, Observed, or Inferred throughout.
standouts: The working pattern is already proven on this machine by the live 8443 -> 5174 tailnet-only HTTPS mapping.
The WSL same-namespace constraint is load-bearing and explicitly verified.
Port collision is flagged as the top trap because 443 and 8443 are already occupied.
conventions: Uses a research-note header with date, machine, versions, and goal.
Includes exact command snippets and verification commands.
Structures content as current state, recommendation, gotchas, and summary.
concerns: A stale socket on 8080 with no owning PID could block OpenWebUI if not cleared.
The 443 funnel exposes a service to the public internet; the file notes it is auth-gated but does not audit that auth.
The OpenWebUI launch example is a placeholder docker command not verified on this machine.
MagicDNS behavior on the iOS Tailscale app is inferred rather than verified.
notes: A machine-specific Tailscale audit that turns generic tailscale serve instructions into an exact port plan while warning not to clobber the existing 443 and 8443 mappings.
questions: What services are actually running on 127.0.0.1:8772 and 127.0.0.1:5174?
What created the stale *:8080 listener and how should it be cleared?
How will OpenWebUI be launched and auto-started inside WSL2 (docker, systemd, etc.)?
Is the iOS Tailscale app configured to use MagicDNS, and will the phone stay connected?
Is WSL2 configured to keep running so the tailnet endpoint remains available?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose is to assess current state and expose OpenWebUI to the phone; the file does exactly that, plus a detailed gotcha list.
apparent_themes: Private mobile access over a tailnet
intention_signals: The file is trying to give a safe, reproducible, one-command path to add OpenWebUI mobile access without breaking existing Tailscale serve/funnel services.
novelty: It maps generic Tailscale serve documentation onto the specific occupied-port reality of this machine, including a stale 8080 socket and existing 443/8443 mappings, producing a concrete port plan rather than a copy-paste example.
contribution: A local audit of Tailscale serve/funnel occupancy and WSL namespace constraints, plus a tailored command and gotcha list for adding a new HTTPS tailnet endpoint for OpenWebUI.
summary_for_embedding: A research note recording the Tailscale tailnet state on a WSL2 workstation and prescribing a new tailnet-only HTTPS serve mapping to expose OpenWebUI to an iPhone without disturbing existing 443 and 8443 mappings.
intention_for_embedding: To provide a safe, verified, machine-specific procedure for adding OpenWebUI as a private, PWA-installable HTTPS endpoint on the existing tailnet.
